elements.sent-2411-001: Given a compact interval $[a,b] \subset [0,\pi]$, we construct a parabolic self-map of the upper half-plane whose set of slopes is $[a,b]$.
elements.sent-2411-002: The nature of this construction is completely discrete and explicit: we explicitly construct a self-map and we explicitly show in which way its orbits wander towards the Denjoy-Wolff point.
elements.sent-2411-003: We also analyze some properties of the Herglotz measure corresponding to such example, which yield the regularity of such self-map in its Denjoy-Wolff point.
elements.sent-2411-004: Discrete Iteration in the unit disk $\D$ is a branch of the vast field known as Complex Dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-005: Given a holomorphic self-map $g \colon \D \to \D$, the main aim is to analyze asymptotic properties of the sequence of iterated self-compositions of $g$, given by $g^0 = \mathrm{Id _{\D}$, and $g^{n+1 = g^n \circ g$, $n \in \N \cup \{0\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-006: In this article we focus on non-elliptic self-maps, which are the ones that possess no fixed point on $\D$.
elements.sent-2411-007: The following well-known result concerns the dynamics of such self-maps.
elements.sent-2411-009: Then, there exists $\tau \in \partial\D$ such that $g^n \to \tau$ locally uniformly, as $n \to \infty$.
elements.sent-2411-010: The point $\tau$ is usually known as the Denjoy-Wolff point of $g$.
elements.sent-2411-011: In particular, if $w_0 \in \D$, the Denjoy-Wolff Theorem proves that the orbit $w_n = g^n(w_0)$ converges to $\tau$, as $n \to \infty$.
elements.sent-2411-012: A classical problem in Discrete Iteration is to characterize the directions through which the orbits of a non-elliptic self-map of $\D$ converge towards the Denjoy-Wolff point.
elements.sent-2411-013: Namely, given $w_0 \in \D$, calculating the numbers $s \in [-\pi/2,\pi/2]$ such that there exists a subsequence of the orbit satisfying $\arg(1-\overline{\tau w_{n_k ) \to s$, as $k \to \infty$.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: This is the Slope Problem, which firstly appeared around 1930 in \cite{Valiron,WolffSlope .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Dealing with this problem is usually easier in the upper half-plane setting.
elements.sent-2411-016: To do so, let $\H := \{z \in \C : \mathrm{Im (z) > 0\}$, and consider the conformal mapping $S \colon \D \to \H$ given by \begin{equation}S(w) = i\dfrac{\tau + w}{\tau - w}, \qquad w \in \D,\end{equation} where $\tau \in \partial\D$ is the Denjoy-Wolff point of $g$.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Then, construct the self-map $f \colon \H \to \H$ given by $f = S \circ g \circ S^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-018: Similarly as in the case of the unit disk, we can consider the iterated self-compositions $f^0 = \mathrm{Id _{\H}$, and $f^{n+1 = f^n \circ f$, $n \in \N \cup \{0\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: It is easy to check that $f^n = S \circ g^n \circ S^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-020: Something similar occurs in the case of orbits.
elements.sent-2411-021: Given $z_0 \in \H$, its orbit by $f$ is the sequence given by $z_n = f^n(z_0)$.
elements.sent-2411-023: If $w_0 \in \D$ and we choose $z_0 = S(w_0)$, then $z_n = S(w_n)$.
elements.sent-2411-025: So that the Denjoy-Wolff point of $f$ is infinity.
elements.sent-2411-026: Concerning the Slope Problem, fixing a subsequence of the orbits, we have that $\arg(1-\overline{\tau w_{n_k ) \to s$ if and only if $\arg(z_n) \to \pi/2-s$.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Inspired by this relationship, we give the following definition.
elements.sent-2411-028: Let $f \colon \H \to \H$ be a holomorphic function whose Denjoy-Wolff point is infinity.
elements.sent-2411-029: Then, given $z_0 \in \H$, we define the set of slopes of $f$ as \begin{equation}Slope[f,z_0] = \{s \in [0,\pi] : There exists a subsequence  \{z_{n_k}\}  with  \arg(z_{n_k}) \to s\},\end{equation} where $\arg$ denotes the principal branch of the argument function.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}Slope[f,z_0] = \{s \in [0,\pi] : There exists a subsequence  \{z_{n_k}\}  with  \arg(z_{n_k}) \to s\},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-030: By \cite[Proposition 2.5.8]{AbateBook , the Denjoy-Wolff point of $f \colon \H \to \H$ is infinity if and only if \begin{equation}\alpha := \angle\lim_{z \to \infty}\dfrac{f(z)}{z} \in [1,+\infty).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-031: In this situation, $f$ is said to be parabolic if $\alpha = 1$, and it is said to be hyperbolic if $\alpha > 1$.
elements.sent-2411-032: Valiron \cite{Valiron studied the Slope Problem for hyperbolic self-maps of $\H$ in 1931.}
elements.sent-2411-033: It turns out that if $f \colon \H \to \H$ is a hyperbolic self-map with Denjoy-Wolff point infinity, then for every $z \in \H$ there exist $\theta \in (0,\pi)$ such that $\lim_{n \to \infty}\arg(f^n(z)) = \theta$; see \cite[Theorem 4.3.4]{AbateBook .}
elements.sent-2411-034: His ideas have been recently revisited by Bracci and Poggi-Corradini.
elements.sent-2411-035: More precisely, they proved that the function $\mathrm{Slope [f,\cdot]\colon \H \to (0,\pi)$ is surjective and harmonic \cite[Property 2 (a) and Property 2 (b)]{BracciCorradiniValiron .}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The theory is a significantly more difficult in the case of parabolic self-maps, which are typically divided in two families.
elements.sent-2411-037: For a holomorphic self-map $f \colon \H \to \H$, the Schwarz-Pick Lemma assures that \begin{equation}k_{\H}(f^{n+2}(z),f^{n+1}(z)) \leq k_{\H}(f^{n+1}(z),f^n(z)), \qquad z \in \H, \, n \in \N,\end{equation} where $k_{\H}$ denotes the hyperbolic distance of the upper half-plane.
elements.sent-2411-038: In particular, $\lim_{n \to \infty k_{\H (f^{n+1 (z),f^n(z))$ exists for all $z \in \H$, and it is a non-negative real number.}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: For non-elliptic self-maps, by \cite[Corollary 4.6.9.(i)]{AbateBook , we know that the latter limit is either positive for every $z \in \H$ or for no $z \in \H$.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Accordingly, we say that $f$ is of positive or of zero hyperbolic step.
elements.sent-2411-041: In 1979, Pommerenke contributed to the Slope Problem for parabolic self-maps of $\H$.
elements.sent-2411-042: In \cite[Remark 1]{PommerenkeHalfPlane , he proved that $\lim_{n \to \infty}\arg(f^n(z))$ exists and it is either $0$ or $\pi$ whenever $f$ is a parabolic self-map of positive hyperbolic step with Denjoy-Wolff point infinity.}
elements.sent-2411-043: In \cite[Proposition 2.6]{CCZRP-Slope , using the hyperbolic geometry, we noticed that the latter limit does not depend on $z$.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Namely, it holds that either $\mathrm{Slope [f,z] = \{0\}$ or $\mathrm{Slope [f,z] = \{\pi\}$ for all $z \in \H$.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The main emphasis in this paper is put on the case where $f$ is a parabolic self-map of zero hyperbolic step.
elements.sent-2411-046: One of the first surprising contributions to this case is in the remarkable paper of Wolff in 1929.
elements.sent-2411-047: In \cite[Section 6]{WolffSlope , he came up with the map $f \colon \H \to \H$ given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:Wolff-example} f(z) = z + ie^{\pi/2}z^i+ie^{\pi/2}, \quad z \in \H,\end{equation} where $z^i$ is defined using the principal branch of the logarithm.}
elements.sent-2411-048: It is easy to check that $f$ is parabolic with Denjoy-Wolff point infinity, since $\angle\lim_{z \to \infty f(z)/z = 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: For this example, Wolff managed to prove that $\mathrm{Slope [f,z_0]$ contains at least two points for all initial points $z_0 \in \H$.}
elements.sent-2411-050: The Slope Problem has also been introduced in the continuous setting of Complex Dynamics, that is, for semigroups $\{\phi_t\}_{t \geq 0}$ of holomorphic self-maps of the upper half-plane.
elements.sent-2411-051: A modern exposition of this topic can be found in \cite[Chapter 17]{BCDM .}
elements.sent-2411-052: In \cite{CDM-Slope-Pacific , Contreras and D\'iaz-Madrigal found that the results of Valiron and Pommerenke can be translated to hyperbolic and parabolic semigroups of positive hyperbolic step, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-053: For a parabolic semigroup of zero hyperbolic step, they proved that the set of slopes is a compact interval which does not depend on the initial point.
elements.sent-2411-054: Despite Wolff's example \eqref{eq:Wolff-example , they conjectured that, in the continuous setting, the set of slopes should always be a singleton.}
elements.sent-2411-055: This conjecture was disproved by Contreras, D\'iaz-Madrigal, and Gumenyuk \cite{CDMGSlope , and also by Betsakos \cite{BetsakosSlope , indepedently.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Namely, they found a semigroup whose set of slopes is the full interval $[0,\pi]$.
elements.sent-2411-057: Improving the techniques by Betsakos, for every prefixed interval $[a,b] \subset [0,\pi]$, Kelgiannis \cite{KelgiannisSlope constructed a semigroup whose set of slopes is $[a,b]$.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Quite recently, the latter ideas were taken back to the discrete setting.
elements.sent-2411-059: Namely, even if the orbits of a self-map are discrete sets, we have shown that the set of slopes of parabolic self-maps of zero hyperbolic step are always a compact interval which does not depend on the initial point.
elements.sent-2411-060: Then, there exists $0 \leq a \leq b \leq \pi$ such that $\mathrm{Slope [f,z] = [a,b]$ for all $z \in \H$.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Examples with arbitrary set of slopes have also been constructed through the use of semigroups.
elements.sent-2411-062: Namely, if $f = \phi_1$, where $\{\phi_t\}$ is the semigroup constructed by Kelgiannis, we have proved that $\mathrm{Slope [f,z] = [a,b]$ for all $z \in \H$.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Therefore, we have the following result.
elements.sent-2411-064: This note focuses on constructing examples of parabolic self-maps of zero hyperbolic step with arbitrary slope sets $[a,b] \subset [0,\pi]$, with $[a,b] \neq [0,\pi]$.
elements.sent-2411-065: These self-maps are of a discrete nature, and so we extend a previous work that primarily relied on semigroups.
elements.sent-2411-066: This yields a new proof of Theorem \ref{thm:arbitrary-slope using tools from Discrete Iteration, which is given in Section \ref{sec:main .}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Indeed, our construction is completely explicit: we explicitly construct the self-map and we explicitly show in which way the orbits wander towards the Denjoy-Wolff point.
elements.sent-2411-068: This strongly contrasts with the arguments in \cite{KelgiannisSlope , where the semigroup is obtained through its Koenigs domain and the proof relies in subtle estimates of some harmonic measures.}
elements.sent-2411-069: In Section \ref{sec:regularity , we analyze some properties of the Herglotz measure corresponding to such examples.}
elements.sent-2411-070: This leads us to discuss the regularity of such self-maps at their Denjoy-Wolff points, and we compare this fact with previous references.
elements.sent-2411-071: In \cite[Section 4]{CCZRP-Slope , we also gave a discrete and explicit construction for the cases $[0,\pi]$ and $[0,\pi/2]$.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Those self-maps are defined through their Herglotz representation formula, and we discuss their regularity at the Denjoy-Wolff point.
elements.sent-2411-073: However, the new construction is easier to follow.
elements.sent-2411-074: The goal of this section is, given $0 \leq a < b \leq \pi$ with $[a,b] \neq [0,\pi]$, to construct a parabolic self-map $f \colon \H \to \H$ of zero hyperbolic step with Denjoy-Wolff point infinity for which \begin{equation}Slope[f,z] = [a,b], \qquad z \in \H.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-075: Notice that the limit case $[a,b] = [0,\pi]$ has been covered in \cite[Theorem 4.2]{CCZRP-Slope with a different technique.}
elements.sent-2411-076: We will start with a general setting, which we appropriately modify later to get the desired examples.
elements.sent-2411-077: To present it, let us fix some notation.
elements.sent-2411-079: We will construct a function $F \colon \Omega \to \C$ such that $F(A_{\theta ) \subset A_{\theta}$.}
elements.sent-2411-080: The function $F$ is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:map-F} F(z) = z + p(z), \qquad z \in \Omega,\end{equation} where $p \colon \Omega \to \C$ is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:map-p} p(z) = \sum_{k = 1}^{\infty}p_k(z), \qquad p_k(z) = \dfrac{a_ke^{i\theta_k}}{(z+\gamma_k)^{\epsilon_k}}, \qquad z \in \Omega,\end{equation} where we are using the main branch of the argument of $w$ to define $w^{\epsilon_k}$.
elements.sent-2411-081: In the definition of $p$, the coefficients $a_k$, $\gamma_k$, $\epsilon_k$ and $\theta_k$ satisfy the following assumptions: \begin{equation}a_k > 0, \qquad \gamma_k > 0, \qquad \epsilon_k > 0, \qquad \pi\epsilon_k \leq \theta, \qquad \lim_{k \to \infty}\epsilon_k = 0, \label{cond:all-positive} \theta_{2k}+\pi\epsilon_{2k} = \theta, \qquad \theta_{2k-1}-\pi\epsilon_{2k-1} = -\theta, \label{cond:angles} \sum_{l = 1}^{\infty}\dfrac{a_l}{\gamma_l^{\epsilon_l}} \leq \dfrac{\gamma_1}{2}, \label{cond:step} \gamma_1 \geq 2, \qquad \gamma_{k+1} > \gamma_k^2, \label{cond:big-gamma} \sum_{l = 1}^{k-1}\dfrac{a_l}{\gamma_k^{\epsilon_l}} \leq \dfrac{\Delta(\theta)a_k}{2k\gamma_k^{2\epsilon_k}}, \qquad \sum_{l = k+1}^{\infty}\dfrac{a_l}{\gamma_l^{\epsilon_l}} \leq \dfrac{\Delta(\theta)a_k}{2k\gamma_k^{2\epsilon_k}}, \label{cond:small-arg}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\Delta(\theta) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{4+\tan^2(\theta)}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-082: The latter conditions are fulfilled for the coefficients \begin{equation}\epsilon_k = \dfrac{\theta}{\pi}\dfrac{1}{2^k}, \qquad a_k = C_1^k(k!)^2, \qquad \gamma_k^{\epsilon_k} = (C_2 k!)^{3^k}, \qquad k \in \N,\end{equation} where $C_1,C_2 > 1$ are large enough constants, and $\theta_k$ is automatically defined by \eqref{cond:angles .}
elements.sent-2411-084: Then, $p$ is a well-defined holomorphic function on $\Omega$.
elements.sent-2411-085: Moreover, the image of $p$ is contained in $A_{\theta}$.

elements.sent-2411-141: Consider the angle \begin{equation}\theta = \dfrac{b-a}{2} \in (0,\pi/2),\end{equation} and the map $F$ as in \eqref{eq:map-F , where we are assuming \eqref{cond:all-positive -\eqref{cond:small-arg .}}}
elements.sent-2411-142: Let us also consider the half-plane $H = \{z \in \C : \arg(z) \in (-\theta-a,\pi-\theta-a)\}$.
elements.sent-2411-144: Then, as seen in Remark \ref{remark:F-self-map , $F(H) \subset H$.}
elements.sent-2411-145: In that case, consider the restriction $g = F_{|H \colon H \to H$.}
elements.sent-2411-146: Let us now remark that the map $p \colon H \to A_{\theta}$, as defined in \eqref{eq:map-p , is bounded.}
elements.sent-2411-147: To see this, notice that \begin{equation}\min_{z \in \partial H}\abs{z+\gamma_k} = \gamma_k\sin(a+\theta).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-148: Then, by \eqref{cond:step , given that $z \in H$, we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:p-is-bounded} \abs{p(z)} \leq \sum_{k = 1}^{\infty}\abs{p_k(z)} \leq \dfrac{1}{\sin(a+\theta)}\sum_{k = 1}^{\infty}\dfrac{a_k}{\gamma_k^{\epsilon_k}} < +\infty,\end{equation} where we have used that $\sin(a+\theta) \in (0,1]$ and $\epsilon_k \leq 1$, by \eqref{cond:all-positive .}}
elements.sent-2411-149: Then, it is clear that \begin{equation}\label{eq:g-is-parabolic} \lim_{\substack{z \to \infty z \in H}}\dfrac{g(z)}{z} = 1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-150: Now, consider the conjugation $f \colon \H \to \H$ given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:def-conjutaged-f} f(z) = \xi g(\overline{\xi} z), \qquad z \in \H ,\end{equation} where $\xi = \exp(i(a+\theta))$.
elements.sent-2411-151: By \eqref{eq:g-is-parabolic , $f$ is also parabolic with Denjoy-Wolff point infinity.}
elements.sent-2411-152: Moreover, it follows from Lemma \ref{lemma:invariant-angle that \begin{equation}Slope[f,z] \subset [a,b], \qquad z \in \H.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-153: In particular, taking $z_0 = \xi\gamma_1/2 \in \H$, Lemma \ref{lemma:main-idea-slope-arbitrary constructs two subsequences of $z_n = f^n(z_0)$ such that \begin{equation}\lim_{k \to \infty}\arg(z_{n_k}) = b, \qquad \lim_{k \to \infty}\arg(z_{m_k}) = a.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-154: Together with Theorem \ref{thm:slope-0hs , this means that \begin{equation}Slope[f,z] = [a,b], \qquad z \in \H.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-155: Since $a < b$, we get that $f$ is of zero hyperbolic step (see \cite[Proposition 2.6]{CCZRP-Slope or \cite[Remark 1]{PommerenkeHalfPlane ).}}
elements.sent-2411-156: We can also prove that the latter examples enjoy some regularity at the Denjoy-Wolff point if further assumptions are made.
elements.sent-2411-157: To do so, we will use the following representation result due to Herglotz.
elements.sent-2411-158: The angular derivative of $f$ at infinity can be characterized in terms of the latter representation.
elements.sent-2411-160: In particular, we see that infinity is the Denjoy-Wolff point of $f$ if and only if $\alpha \geq 1$.
elements.sent-2411-161: Moreover, in this case, $f$ is parabolic if $\alpha = 1$ and hyperbolic if $\alpha > 1$.
elements.sent-2411-162: Remember that, up to a conjugation, one can always suppose that a parabolic map has Denjoy-Wolff point infinity.
elements.sent-2411-163: Therefore, in this sense, the following result can be used to describe the behaviour of all parabolic functions.
elements.sent-2411-164: Using this result, for a parabolic function $f \colon \H \to \H$ with Denjoy-Wolff point infinity, we say that $f$ is {\sl represented by the pair $(\beta,\mu)$ whenever \eqref{eq:Herglotz holds.}}
elements.sent-2411-165: This notation is used in the following proposition.
elements.sent-2411-166: Let $f \colon \H \to \H$ be the parabolic self-map constructed in the proof of Theorem \ref{thm:arbitrary-slope-examples .}
elements.sent-2411-167: Suppose that $f$ is represented by the pair $(\beta,\mu)$.
elements.sent-2411-168: Then, the measure $\mu$ is absolutely continuous with respect to Lebesgue measure on $\R$.
elements.sent-2411-171: A proof of Proposition \ref{prop:regularity-example-slope will be given at the end of the section.}
elements.sent-2411-172: Before doing so, we should discuss the way in which this results shows that the map $f$ constructed in Theorem \ref{thm:arbitrary-slope-examples enjoys some regularity at the Denjoy-Wolff point.}
elements.sent-2411-173: In \cite{CDP-C2 , Contreras, D\'iaz-Madrigal, and Pommerenke studied several dynamical aspects of the iterates of a self-map with additional regularity at its Denjoy-Wolff point.}
elements.sent-2411-174: To introduce their ideas, consider a holomorphic map $\phi \colon \D \to \C$ with a boundary fixed point $\tau \in \T$, that is, \begin{equation}\angle\lim_{z \to \tau}\phi(z) = \tau.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-175: The map $\phi$ is said to be of angular-class of order $p \in \N$ at $\tau$, denoted as $\phi \in C_A^p(\tau)$, if there exist $c_1,\ldots,c_p \in \C$ and a holomorphic function $\gamma \colon \D \to \C$ such that \begin{equation}\phi(z) = \tau + \sum_{k = 1}^p\dfrac{c_k}{k!}(z-\tau)^k+\gamma(z), \quad z \in \D, \quad \angle\lim_{z \to \tau}\dfrac{\gamma(z)}{(z-\tau)^p} = 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-176: In particular, as a consequence of Julia-Wolff-Carath\'eodory Theorem \cite[Corollary 2.5.5]{AbateBook , every non-elliptic function is of angular-class of first order at its Denjoy-Wolff point.}
elements.sent-2411-177: There are some necessary conditions in terms of the Herglotz's representation \eqref{eq:Herglotz to assure that a map enjoys a certain angular regularity.}
elements.sent-2411-178: We use here the following result, which follows from \cite[Proposition 2.1]{CDP-C2 and \cite[Lemma 3.3]{HS .}}
elements.sent-2411-179: It has already been noticed in \cite[Proposition 3.6]{CZ-FiniteShift and \cite[Remark 4.6]{CCZRP-Slope .}}
elements.sent-2411-180: Let $f \colon \H \to \H$ be a parabolic self-map whose Denjoy-Wolff point is infinity, represented by the pair $(\beta,\mu)$.
elements.sent-2411-182: Consider the self-map $\phi \colon \D \to \D$ which is conjugated to $f$ and has Denjoy-Wolff point $\tau \in \partial\D$.
elements.sent-2411-184: Therefore, if we further assume \eqref{cond:sum-for-l1 , the self-map constructed in the proof of Theorem \ref{thm:arbitrary-slope-examples enjoys the regularity presented in the latter proposition.}}
elements.sent-2411-185: It is worth mentioning that there are several previous references in the literature concerning the Slope Problem and the aforementioned regularity.
elements.sent-2411-186: More precisely, such references prove that the orbits of a semigroup have to converge with a definite slope if its Koenigs function or its infinitesimal generator enjoy some regularity.
elements.sent-2411-188: Our constructions are in contrast with such results, since the aforementioned regularity of the self-maps can be related with the regularity of their Koenigs maps, as in \cite[Theorem 4.1]{CDP-C2 , or its derivative, as in \cite[Theorems 5.2 and 6.2]{CDP-C2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-189: For semigroups, the derivative of the Koenigs map is related to the so-called infinitesimal generator, see \cite[Chapter 10]{BCDM .}
elements.sent-2411-190: This is also an improvement in comparison to the examples given in \cite{CDMGSlope,BetsakosSlope,KelgiannisSlope , where the regularity of the associated semigroup is unclear.}
elements.sent-2411-191: Let us also notice that the example by Wolff given in \eqref{eq:Wolff-example is not regular in the above sense.}
elements.sent-2411-192: Notice that $v$ is a harmonic map on $\H$.
elements.sent-2411-194: Therefore, it can be represented as the Poisson integral of its boundary values \cite[Chapter I, Lemma 3.4]{Garnett-Bounded , that is, \begin{equation}v(z) = \int_{\R}\dfrac{1}{\pi}\dfrac{y}{(t-x)^2+y^2}v^*(t)dt, \qquad z = x+iy \in \H,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}v^*(x) = \lim_{y \to 0^+}v(x+iy) \in [0,+\infty).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-195: By Remark \ref{remark:p-is-holomorphic , $f$ is holomorphic in a domain containing $\overline{\H}$.}
elements.sent-2411-196: Then, the latter limit exists for all $x \in \R$.. Recall that \begin{equation}Im(f(z)-z) = v(z) = \int_{\R}\dfrac{(1+t^2)y}{(t-x)^2+y^2}d\mu(t), \qquad z \in \H.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-197: Then, using the uniqueness in Theorem \ref{thm:Herglotz-general , we have that \begin{equation}\dfrac{d\mu}{dm}(t) = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\dfrac{v^*(t)}{1+t^2}, \quad t \in \R,\end{equation} where $m$ is Lebesgue measure on $\R$.}
elements.sent-2411-198: Let us now prove that, if \eqref{cond:sum-for-l1 holds, then \begin{equation}\int_{\R}\abs{t}d\mu(t) < +\infty.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-199: To do so, using \eqref{eq:def-conjutaged-f , we note that \begin{equation}\label{eq:int-to-sums} \int_{\R}\abs{t}d\mu(t) = \int_{\R}\dfrac{\abs{t}}{1+t^2}v^*(t)dt \leq \int_{\R}\dfrac{\abs{t}}{1+t^2}\abs{p(\overline{\xi}t)}dt \leq \sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\int_{\R}\dfrac{a_k\abs{t}}{(1+t^2)\abs{\overline{\xi}t+\gamma_k}^{\epsilon_k}}dt. \nonumber\end{equation} Let us estimate the latter integrals.}
elements.sent-2411-200: To start, notice that \begin{equation}\int_{\R}\dfrac{\abs{t}}{(1+t^2)\abs{\overline{\xi}t+\gamma_k}^{\epsilon_k}}dt = \int_{\R}\dfrac{\abs{t}}{(1+t^2)\abs{t+\cos(\phi)\gamma_k+i\sin(\phi)\gamma_k}^{\epsilon_k}}dt,\end{equation} where we recall that $\xi = e^{i\phi}$, $\phi = a + \theta$, and $\theta = (b-a)/2$.
elements.sent-2411-201: Then, since $0 \leq a < b \leq \pi$, we conclude that $\phi \in (0,\pi)$.
elements.sent-2411-202: For the estimates, we assume that $\phi \in (0,\pi/2]$ (the other case is similar).
elements.sent-2411-203: Indeed, we split the latter integral into three parts.
elements.sent-2411-204: For the first one, we have \begin{equation}\int_{-\gamma_k}^{\gamma_k}\dfrac{\abs{t}}{(1+t^2)\abs{t+\cos(\phi)\gamma_k+i\sin(\phi)\gamma_k}^{\epsilon_k}}dt \leq \int_{-\gamma_k}^{\gamma_k}\dfrac{\abs{t}}{(1+t^2)\sin(\phi)^{\epsilon_k}\gamma_k^{\epsilon_k}}dt = \dfrac{\log(1+\gamma_k^2)}{\sin(\phi)^{\epsilon_k}\gamma_k^{\epsilon_k}} \leq \dfrac{\log(1+\gamma_k^2)}{\sin(\phi)\gamma_k^{\epsilon_k}},\end{equation} where we have used that $\sin(\phi) \in (0,1]$ and $\epsilon_k \in (0,1)$.
elements.sent-2411-206: For the third part, we have that \begin{equation}\int_{-\infty}^{-\gamma_k}\dfrac{\abs{t}}{(1+t^2)\abs{t+\cos(\phi)\gamma_k+i\sin(\phi)\gamma_k}^{\epsilon_k}}dt = \int_{\gamma_k}^{+\infty}\dfrac{t}{(1+t^2)\abs{-t+\cos(\phi)\gamma_k+i\sin(\phi)\gamma_k}^{\epsilon_k}}dt \leq \int_{\gamma_k}^{+\infty}\dfrac{t}{(1+t^2)(t-\cos(\phi)\gamma_k)^{\epsilon_k}}dt.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-207: However, note that \begin{equation}t \mapsto \dfrac{t}{t-\cos(\phi)\gamma_k}, \qquad t \geq \gamma_k,\end{equation} is a decreasing map.
elements.sent-2411-209: This means that \begin{equation}\int_{-\infty}^{-\gamma_k}\dfrac{\abs{t}}{(1+t^2)\abs{t+\cos(\phi)\gamma_k+i\sin(\phi)\gamma_k}^{\epsilon_k}}dt \leq \dfrac{1}{(1-\cos(\phi))^{\epsilon_k}}\int_{\gamma_k}^{+\infty}\dfrac{dt}{t^{1+\epsilon_k}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-\cos(\phi))^{\epsilon_k}}\dfrac{1}{\epsilon_k\gamma_k^{\epsilon_k}} \leq \dfrac{1}{1-\cos(\phi)}\dfrac{1}{\epsilon_k\gamma_k^{\epsilon_k}},\end{equation} where we have used that $1-\cos(\phi) \in (0,1]$ and $\epsilon_k \in (0,1)$.
elements.sent-2411-210: All in all, we have shown that there exists $C = C(\phi) > 0$ so that, using \eqref{eq:int-to-sums , we have that \begin{equation}\int_{\R}\abs{t}d\mu(t) \leq C(\phi)\sum_{k = 1}^{\infty}\left(\dfrac{a_k\log(1+\gamma_k^2)}{\gamma_k^{\epsilon_k}} + \dfrac{a_k}{\epsilon_k\gamma_k^{\epsilon_k}}\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-212: Finally, we notice that if \begin{equation}\int_{\R}\abs{t}d\mu(t) < +\infty,\end{equation} then the equality \begin{equation}\beta = \int_{\R}td\mu(t)\end{equation} is a consequence of \cite[Theorem 3.4]{CCZRP-Slope .}
